- Add `--shadow-tab-active: inset 0 -4px 0 var(--yellow)`
- Add `--shadow-focus-pink: var(--shadow-sm), 0 0 0 2px var(--pink)`

Spacing / density:
- Add primitive exact spacing tokens for repeated visual rhythm: `--space-0`, `--space-1`, `--space-2`, `--space-3`, `--space-4`, `--space-5`, `--space-6`, `--space-7`, `--space-8`, `--space-9`, `--space-10`, `--space-11`, `--space-12`, `--space-14`, `--space-16`, `--space-18`, `--space-20`, `--space-22`, `--space-24`, `--space-30`, `--space-38`, `--space-40`, `--space-50`, `--space-56`.
- Add semantic composites for common visual spacing:
  - `--space-row-gap: var(--space-14)`
  - `--space-row-gap-compact: var(--space-10)`
  - `--space-bubble-pad: 12px 16px`
  - `--space-thread-bubble-pad: 7px 11px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-xs: 1px 5px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-sm: 1px 6px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-md: 2px 7px`
  - `--space-sticker-pad: 4px 10px`
  - `--space-button-pad-sm: 4px 8px`
  - `--space-button-pad-md: 8px 12px`
  - `--density-cozy-pad: 16px 20px 14px`
  - `--density-compact-pad: 10px 16px 12px`

2. Literal-to-token mapping table

CSS radii:
- `0` -> `var(--radius-none)`
- `1px` -> `var(--radius-hair)`
- `3px` -> `var(--radius-xs)`
- `4px` -> `var(--radius-sm)`
- `5px` -> `var(--radius-md)`
- `6px` -> `var(--radius-lg)`
- `8px` -> `var(--radius-xl)`
- `10px` -> `var(--radius-2xl)`
- `999px` -> `var(--radius-pill)`
- `6px 6px 0 0` -> `var(--radius-panel-top)`
- `0 0 6px 6px` -> `var(--radius-panel-bottom)`

CSS borders:
- `3px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-bold)` or existing `var(--line)`
- `2.5px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-md)`
- `2px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-sm)` or existing `var(--line-2)`
- `1.5px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-xs)`
- `1px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-hair)`
- `none` -> `var(--line-none)`
- `2px solid transparent` -> `var(--line-transparent)`
- `2.5px solid transparent` -> `var(--line-md-transparent)`
- `2.5px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-md)`
- `2px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-sm)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-xs)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--ink-faint)` -> `var(--line-dashed-faint)`
- `2px dashed var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-rule-dashed-sm)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-rule-dashed-xs)`

CSS shadows:
- `none` -> `var(--shadow-none)`
- `0.75px 0.75px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-xxs)`
- `1px 1px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-xs)`
- `1.5px 1.5px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-chip)`
- `2px 2px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-sm)`
- `2.5px 2.5px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-hover-sm)`
- `3px 3px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-hover)`
- `4px 4px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-md)` or existing `var(--shadow)`
- `6px 6px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-lg)`
- pink/focus/inset special cases -> the named accent shadow tokens above.

CSS typography:
- `"Archivo Black", sans-serif` -> `var(--font-display)`
- `"Space Grotesk", "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif` and `"Space Grotesk", sans-serif` -> `var(--font-ui)`
- `"JetBrains Mono", monospace` -> `var(--font-mono)`
- px font sizes from 9px through 24px map to the exact `--text-*` token above.
- letter spacing values map to `--tracking-*` above.

CSS spacing:
- Repeated single-value gaps/paddings map to exact `--space-*` tokens.
- Common repeated multi-value padding maps to semantic composite tokens above.
- One-off layout-specific padding may stay literal only if it is documented as structural layout math in the implementation bead notes.

TSX inline style leaks:
- `MessageRow.tsx` author chip: static `fontFamily`, `fontSize`, `letterSpacing`, `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow` map to `var(--font-display)`, `var(--text-12)`, `var(--tracking-xs)`, `var(--space-chip-pad-author)` (or exact composite `3px 9px` if added), `var(--line-sm)`, `var(--radius-md)`, `var(--shadow-sm)`.
- `ThreadPane.tsx` parent author chip: static `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow`, `fontFamily`, `fontSize` map to token equivalents. `background: parentAuthor.color` remains dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` Avatar: static `borderRadius`, `border`, `fontFamily`, `boxShadow` map to tokens. `width`, `height`, `background`, `color`, `fontSize: Math.round(size * 0.45)` remain dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` StatusDot: static `borderRadius`, `border` map to tokens. `width`, `height`, `background`, and conditional `animation` remain dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` Sticker: static `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow`, `fontFamily`, `fontSize`, `letterSpacing`, `color` map to tokens. `background: color ?? ...` and `transform: rotate(${tilt}deg)` remain dynamic/intentional.

3. Intentional exceptions

Do not tokenize these in the sweep unless a later validation screenshot proves they are theme blockers:
- Structural layout dimensions: sidebar width, timeline width, roster width, thread-pane clamps, grid column definitions, avatar/tile dimensions, scrollbar widths, virtualizer heights/transforms, absolute offsets, negative margins used for grouped messages or avatar stacks.
- Dynamic data styles: agent colors, mention colors, computed positions, computed percentages, virtualizer `height` / `translateY`, `StatusDot` status animation selection, `Sticker` tilt, `Avatar` size-derived font size.
- Code block colors: `--code-bg` / `--code-fg` intentionally stay dark in both light and dark themes per `web/DESIGN.md`.
- Component-specific geometry that encodes shape rather than theme, such as tooltip arrows and timeline marker alignment comments, can remain literal if documented in the relevant child bead.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Mapping table committed to this bead's --append-notes or --design field. (2) Every literal value the audit flagged has a destination token. (3) Naming scheme reviewed and final — no renames during the implementation sweeps. (4) Identifies any literals that should INTENTIONALLY stay literal (e.g. magic numbers tied to specific layout math — document each exception with rationale).
notes: Audit correction before implementation: add two TSX chip composite tokens to the frozen list: --space-author-chip-pad: 3px 9px for MessageRow author chips, and --space-thread-author-chip-pad: 2px 8px for ThreadPane parent author chips. Use these instead of the provisional name --space-chip-pad-author in the design text. This is a naming correction made before any implementation sweep starts.
close_reason: Completed no-code token audit and finalized mapping/naming scheme in bead design/notes. Baseline screenshots captured in .codex-artifacts/ui-token-baseline.

title: Web UI: tokenize design system for multi-theme readiness
description: Migrate the web UI's visual decisions (radii, shadows, borders, spacing, typography) from literal CSS/TSX values to CSS custom-prop tokens, so that future aesthetic themes (vanilla Slack/WhatsApp-style minimal, etc.) can be added as a single :root override block — without changing component code. Driven by the audit dated 2026-05-23: components already use semantic class names and rarely hardcode aesthetic values; the remaining literals are concentrated in styles.css/extra.css (~116 raw values) and ~17 TSX inline-style leaks. This epic finishes that job. Out of scope for this epic: Radix accessibility migration (separate epic, deferred behind validating this lands cleanly); shipping any actual second theme (deferred until a concrete second design exists). The neo-brutalist default theme is not changed visually by this work — see the Final Verification sub-issue.
design: Architecture: Option A from the 2026-05-23 design discussion. Pure tokens + :root overrides. Rejected alternatives: Option B (per-theme override stylesheets) deferred until a real second mock exists; Option C (CVA / variant-per-component) rejected as invasive and unjustified given speculative theme count. Audit numbers grounding the scope: 25 inline style={} usages across 10 components, of which only ~17 leak literal aesthetic values (rest pass dynamic data — agent color, position, computed percent — and stay); raw hex colors in TSX = 14 of which 12 are the AgentColorPicker palette swatches (acceptable, they ARE the palette); 182 className= usages, all semantic. CSS bulk: extra.css ~116 raw visual-value declarations, mixed token discipline (some use var(--shadow), others hardcode '2px 2px 0 var(--rule)'); radii not tokenized; DESIGN.md's rounded scale exists conceptually but lacks --radius-* CSS vars. The 'switch theme on click' UX is enabled by toggling document.documentElement.dataset.theme; same mechanism as the existing light/dark toggle.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Every child issue closed. (2) bun test green; bun run typecheck green. (3) Default neo-brutalist theme is visually identical to baseline (master at epic-start) — verified via the Final Verification sub-issue. (4) A throwaway alternate-theme [data-theme=...] block exists (or existed during validation) that demonstrates every component re-skins on attribute swap without layout breakage. (5) web/DESIGN.md documents the token contract so a future contributor knows which side of the inline-vs-CSS-vs-token line a value belongs on.

title: Test real Pi MCP group join mention and thread workflow
description:
What/why/how:
Add a real Pi AoE/tmux MCP smoke scenario for group-policy v0. The scenario should use isolated per-worktree Pi config/auth like the existing Pi DM smoke, launch at least two Pi agents, wait for auto-registration, then prompt one or more agents to use synchronize MCP tools for a group workflow. Keep prompts high-level and self-discovery based.

Impact area:
- New real Pi scenario under scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/.

- MCP group tools: bridge_whoami, bridge_list_peers, bridge_join_group, bridge_send_group, bridge_group_history, and optionally bridge_rename_in_group.

Acceptance criteria:
- Two Pi agents launch in AoE/tmux with isolated Pi home/session dirs and current-worktree MCP config.
- The sender Pi calls bridge_whoami and bridge_list_peers before choosing another live Pi peer or group participant.
- Pi agents join a test group through bridge_join_group without being spoon-fed their own peer_id/session name.
- A Pi agent sends a group message with an @alias mention and then sends or reads a thread reply via MCP tools.
- REST assertions confirm group membership, group_message events, mentions_json or thread parent_event_id, and history visibility.
- Transcript evidence contains the expected MCP tool calls and no shell/CLI fallback for the tested workflow.
- --keep preserves AoE profile and run state for manual inspection.

How to verify:

- Inspect artifacts: pi-peers.json, transcript evidence, group history JSON, pane captures.
- Run with --keep once and attach through AoE for manual validation.

Test plan:

- Deterministic fake-shell issues provide the stable regression layer; this verifies real-agent tool use.

Dependencies:
- Scaffold group-policy scenario entry points
- Test CLI group threads mentions and inbox routing

Improvements:
- Later expand to three Pi agents to make mention routing and thread participant behavior more realistic.
close_reason: Implemented real Pi AoE/tmux group-policy MCP workflow; validated warmup, self-discovery prompts, group aliases, mention resolution, thread reply state, and transcript evidence.

title: Test CLI group threads mentions and inbox routing
description:

Acceptance criteria:
- Three fake agents join one group with aliases alice, bob, and carol.
- A root message is sent, then a reply, then a reply-to-reply; REST confirms replies collapse to the root event_id.
- Default group history hides thread replies; thread history returns root plus replies in chronological order.
- A main-channel message mentioning @bob and @ghost returns an unresolved warning for @ghost and stores bob's peer_id in mentions_json.
- Inbox assertions confirm non-sender active members receive durable group message rows regardless of mention status.
- Roster events are either filtered out intentionally in message assertions or separately asserted as durable inbox events.

How to verify:
- Run the deterministic group-policy wrapper and inspect the REST validation artifact.
- Confirm command transcripts show CLI usage rather than direct REST mutation for workflow actions.
- Existing tests/api.test.ts mention/thread tests still pass.

- Optional push-sink coverage can be added if the harness scaffolding exposes a stable local callback server.

Improvements:

title: Accept peer_id alias for bridge_dm MCP calls

title: Render alias#<suffix> in TUI and web UI rosters/history
description: Daemon now exposes host_session_id on every group_members row and peer_id is already canonical. UI consumers should render the canonical disambiguator alongside alias everywhere a member or sender is shown: prefer alias#<host_session_id[0:6]> when bound, otherwise alias#<peer_id[0:4]>. Targets: TUI top/status output (src/cli/render/), the web chat roster (web/src/components/AgentRoster.tsx and any message-author rendering), and group history events that surface a sender. This is the deferred display piece from Phase 1 (sync-2l1) and supersedes sync-0oc (duplicate session names in top/status).
design: Daemon already returns host_session_id on member rows via MEMBER_SELECT_SQL. UI layers compute a deterministic short suffix and append it. No daemon-side changes required. Test by running multiple peers with the same session_name (and/or one bound + one cli fallback) and confirming the rendered names visually distinct.
acceptance_criteria: TUI peer/member listings show suffix; web roster shows suffix; duplicate session_name + same host_session_id is impossible by construction so the suffix never collides; sync-0oc verified closed by this rendering.
close_reason: TUI portion shipped on worktree-sync-dx2-tui-suffix. SummaryPeer now carries host_session_id; renderSummary composes alias#<suffix> for every peer (host_session_id[0:6] when bound, peer_id[0:4] otherwise). 27 tests pass. Web UI rendering is structurally blocked by sync-jix (DaemonDataSource is still a throwing stub); when sync-jix lands, the same peerDisplayName helper can be reused. Closing because the actionable TUI work is done — file a fresh bead under sync-jix if/when web rendering needs separate tracking.

title: Group policy phase 3b: mentions + notification routing
description: Rewrite the group message notification fanout to be mention-aware and thread-aware. Add events.mentions_json TEXT NULL column storing resolved peer_ids at send time. Daemon parses @-tokens, resolves each via group_members.alias (active=1 only), and returns a warnings array for unresolved tokens (alias_not_in_group). The message still sends -- warnings are non-fatal. Push fanout rules: main channel msg -> push to mentions only; thread reply -> push to root_author U thread_posters U new mentions; group_joined/left/group_member_alias_reclaimed -> inbox only, no push. Inbox rows always written for every active member regardless of push. Bundles tightly with phase 3a (threads) because both rewrite notifySubscribers; recommend keeping them in adjacent worktrees with a shared base branch.
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md sections "Mentions & notification routing," "Message routing in main channel," "Message routing in a thread," and the routing table. Schema: ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN mentions_json. API: bridge_send_group response shape becomes { event, warnings? }. Touchpoints: src/daemon.ts (notifySubscribers rewrite + mention resolver), src/api/groups.ts (response type), src/mcp/tools/groups.ts (warnings passthrough), tests for each routing rule.

title: Replace mention-autocomplete popup with Radix Popover + cmdk
description:
## Why
\`web/src/components/Composer.tsx\` has a homegrown \`@mention\` autocomplete:
- Custom \`useLayoutEffect\` + \`getBoundingClientRect\` positioning (works, but fragile on resize).
- Arrow / Enter / Tab / Escape keyboard handlers tangled into the textarea \`onKeyDown\`.
- No \`combobox\` ARIA pattern — screen readers don't know it's an autocomplete.
- No focus management between textarea and popup.
- Filter logic is hand-rolled \`startsWith\` matching.

A proper \`combobox\` is one of the trickiest a11y patterns to implement correctly — exactly the kind of thing to outsource.

## Approach
Use \`@radix-ui/react-popover\` for positioning + \`cmdk\` (the library shadcn's Command uses) for the filtered list and keyboard handling.

\`\`\`tsx
<Popover.Root open={mentionQuery !== null}>
  <Popover.Anchor asChild><textarea ... /></Popover.Anchor>
  <Popover.Content side="top" align="start" sideOffset={8}>
    <Command>
      <Command.List>
        {candidates.map(a => <Command.Item key={a.id} ...>...</Command.Item>)}
      </Command.List>
    </Command>
  </Popover.Content>
</Popover.Root>
\`\`\`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Mention popup uses Radix Popover for positioning (no more hand-rolled \`getBoundingClientRect\`).
- [ ] \`cmdk\` handles arrow keys, Enter, Escape, filtering. The textarea \`onKeyDown\` is freed from mention-specific branches.
- [ ] Trigger has \`role="combobox"\`, popup has \`role="listbox"\`, items have \`role="option"\` (cmdk provides this).
- [ ] Selecting an item commits the mention at the current caret (existing \`commitMention\` logic preserved).
- [ ] Popup never gets clipped (Radix portals to \`<body>\`).
- [ ] Visual styling preserved (\`.mention-pop\`, \`.mention-row\` classes).
- [ ] Works inside both the main \`ChatView\` composer and the \`ThreadPane\` composer.

## Complex pieces
- **Caret tracking.** The popup anchors to the textarea, not to the caret. For now, anchoring to the textarea (current behavior) is acceptable; "anchor to caret" is a follow-up.
- **Controlled-open state.** Radix Popover is controlled or uncontrolled. We need controlled because the open state is driven by \"\`@\` was typed at caret\". Wire \`open={mentionQuery !== null}\` + \`onOpenChange\`.
- **Composer collapse.** When the composer is collapsed via the new \`▼\` button, the popup must close. Easy via \`onOpenChange\` + the \`collapsed\` state.

## Estimate
4–6 h.

title: Replace homegrown ContextMenu with Radix (a11y win)
description:
## Why
\`web/src/components/ContextMenu.tsx\` is a 70-line homegrown context menu used by four surfaces (Sidebar room items, MessageRow, AgentRoster cards — future TimelineRail nodes). It works visually but has **zero accessibility**:
- No keyboard navigation (arrow keys don't move focus through items).
- No focus trap when open.
- No \`aria-haspopup\` / \`aria-expanded\` / \`role="menu"\` wiring on the trigger.
- No screen-reader announcement of menu open/close.
- Submenus would be impossible to add safely.

This is load-bearing for any user on keyboard or assistive tech.

## Approach
Replace with \`@radix-ui/react-context-menu\` (works headless — bring our own brutalist CSS). No Tailwind required for this slice.

\`\`\`tsx
<ContextMenu.Root>
  <ContextMenu.Trigger asChild>{children}</ContextMenu.Trigger>
  <ContextMenu.Portal>
    <ContextMenu.Content className="ctx-menu">
      <ContextMenu.Item onSelect={...}>Reply in thread</ContextMenu.Item>
      <ContextMenu.Separator />
      <ContextMenu.Item className="ctx-danger" onSelect={...}>Delete</ContextMenu.Item>
    </ContextMenu.Content>
  </ContextMenu.Portal>
</ContextMenu.Root>
\`\`\`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All four context-menu surfaces (Sidebar room item, MessageRow, AgentRoster card, planned TimelineRail node) use Radix \`ContextMenu.Root\`.
- [ ] Keyboard: arrow keys navigate items, Enter selects, Escape closes, focus returns to trigger.
- [ ] ARIA: trigger gets \`aria-haspopup="menu"\`, content has \`role="menu"\`, items have \`role="menuitem"\` (Radix handles this).
- [ ] Existing brutalist styling preserved (\`.ctx-menu\`, \`.ctx-item\`, \`.ctx-divider\`, \`.ctx-danger\` classes).
- [ ] No visual regression in light or dark mode.
- [ ] Bundle delta < 15 kB gzipped.

## Complex pieces
- **\`useContextMenu()\` hook API change.** Today: \`openMenu(event, items[])\` imperative. Radix is declarative — wrap children in \`<ContextMenu.Root>\`. Need to refactor the four call sites.
- **Items as data, not JSX.** Current callers pass an array of \`MenuEntry\` objects. Either keep that shape and wrap a small \`<ContextMenuFromItems items={...}>\` helper, or refactor each call site to inline \`<ContextMenu.Item>\` JSX (cleaner).
- **Portal rendering.** Radix portals to \`document.body\` by default. Verify it doesn't get clipped behind \`.tab-content { overflow: hidden }\` (it shouldn't — portals escape).

## Estimate
3–4 h.

title: Web chat UI for synchronize agent sessions
description:
## Goal
Build a custom web UI that renders all synchronize agent sessions (Claude, Codex, Pi, CLI) and their interactions — DMs, group threads, inbox, media — in a single browser surface.

## Why
Today the only way to observe live agent activity across sessions is the CLI (`synchronize top`, `synchronize inbox`) or by tailing logs / poking the daemon REST API. A web UI gives a richer, persistent view: chronological message timelines per peer/group, live event stream, media previews, group membership, identity attribution (claude / codex / pi / cli), and history search.

## Scope (high-level — break into child issues before implementing)
- Daemon-side: confirm the REST surface in `src/api/` is sufficient for read paths; add SSE/WebSocket event stream if polling is insufficient.
- Frontend: standalone web app (likely Bun + Vite + React or similar — decide before implementation). Talks to the daemon over the existing REST API plus a live event channel.
- Auth/binding: stays local-first; only loopback by default. LAN mode reuses `SYNCHRONIZE_TOKEN`.
- Views (minimum): peers list with tool attribution, per-peer DM thread, group list + thread + history, inbox, media gallery, live event firehose.
- Packaging: a `make web` / `make install-web` target consistent with the existing install matrix.

## Non-goals (v0)
- No write/send from the UI initially — read-only observer first; sending is a follow-up epic.
- No remote/cloud sync, no auth beyond the daemon's existing bind+token model.
- No theming/extensibility framework.

## Out of this epic
- Child issues for: tech-stack decision, REST/SSE gap analysis, frontend scaffold, each view, install/packaging, manual smoke.

title: Split daemon repository media and subscription helpers
description:
## Context
Beyond route handling, `src/daemon.ts` contains database read/write helpers, row formatting, media hash/copy/index behavior, and subscriber callback fanout. These are distinct responsibilities and should be isolated after route boundaries are stable.

## Specific objective
Extract daemon persistence, media-store, and subscription delivery helpers into focused modules without changing database schema or runtime behavior.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`
- New `src/daemon/repo/*` modules for peers/events/groups/media helpers
- New `src/daemon/media-store.ts`
- New `src/daemon/subscriptions.ts`
- Existing DB and media behavior used by REST routes and MCP callback delivery

## Acceptance criteria
- Database row interfaces and helper functions are grouped by domain.
- Media copy/hash/index/README behavior is isolated and unchanged.
- Subscription callback fanout and delivered_at semantics are isolated and unchanged.
- No schema or migration change is introduced.
- Tests continue to prove DM, groups, media, inbox, and MCP channel delivery behavior.

## How to verify
- Run `bun run typecheck`.
- Run `bun test tests/messaging.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test` after the split.

## Test plan
Existing integration tests are the primary guard because this issue touches persistence and event delivery. Add focused tests only if extraction reveals untested behavior.

## Dependencies
- Daemon route/validation split issue.
acceptance_criteria: Database helper functions, media-store filesystem helpers, and subscription fanout are separated from route handlers; behavior remains unchanged under full tests.

title: Split daemon route handlers and validation helpers
description:
## Context
`src/daemon.ts` currently has a single large route function with all endpoint matching, body parsing, validation, and response construction. Splitting route handlers will make future REST changes safer and easier to review.

## Specific objective
Extract daemon route dispatch and validation helpers into `src/daemon/` modules without changing endpoint behavior.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`

title: Design daemon module boundaries for route persistence and media
description:
## Context
`src/daemon.ts` is the largest TypeScript file in the repo at roughly 1077 lines. It mixes REST route dispatch, request validation, database row types/helpers, subscription fanout, media-store filesystem behavior, daemon startup, auth, and response/error handling. This should be modularized, but it is higher risk than API/CLI/MCP splitting because it owns the core persistence and delivery behavior.

## Specific objective
Design daemon module boundaries before moving code. This issue should produce a clear plan for splitting `src/daemon.ts` while preserving the daemon REST contract and all current tests.

## Suggested target shape
```text
src/daemon.ts -> compatibility executable entrypoint
src/daemon/
  index.ts or server.ts       startup and Bun.serve wiring
  context.ts                  DaemonContext and setup
  auth.ts                     bind/token validation and auth checks
  validation.ts               request body/query validation helpers
  routes.ts                   top-level route dispatcher
  routes/
    health.ts
    status.ts
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
    subscriptions.ts
    events.ts
  repo/
    peers.ts
    events.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
  media-store.ts              file copy/hash/index/readme behavior
  subscriptions.ts            callback fanout and delivery marking
```

The final implementation can adjust this if simpler boundaries emerge, but route handling, persistence helpers, subscription fanout, and media filesystem logic should not remain tangled in one file.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`
- Future `src/daemon/*` modules
- Tests covering REST, messaging, MCP callback subscription, media, and health/status

## Acceptance criteria
- A daemon module map is captured in notes/design.
- The plan identifies route modules, shared validation/auth helpers, repository helpers, subscription fanout, and media-store helpers.
- The plan explicitly preserves the REST API contract and database behavior.
- The plan defines verification after each daemon split stage.

## How to verify
- Inspect `src/daemon.ts` section boundaries and confirm no planned import cycles.
- Run `bun run typecheck` before any implementation starts.
- No runtime behavior should change in this design-only issue.

## Test plan
No runtime tests required unless scaffolding is introduced. If scaffolding is introduced, run `bun run typecheck`.

## Dependencies
- Overall module boundary design issue.
acceptance_criteria: Daemon split boundaries are documented before code movement; route, validation, persistence, subscription, media, and startup responsibilities are assigned to modules; no code behavior changes required.
notes: Design doc: docs/daemon-modularization-plan.md (worktree-refactor-daemon-design). Covers eight current concerns, target module map including the missed web/* group (file grew 1077→2169 since epic was filed), import direction, route-precedence overlap table, in-scope vs deferred duplications (zod fragments → sync-3wc; DB-row↔api/types.ts dedup → follow-up; route boilerplate wrapper → defer), and per-phase verification gate. Phase A = sync-mkj.10 (routes/validation/auth), Phase B = sync-mkj.11 (repo/media/subscriptions/web).

title: Modularize CLI MCP and API TypeScript structure
description:
## Goal
Refactor the synchronized TypeScript code layout from large single files (`src/api.ts`, `src/cli.ts`, `src/mcp.ts`) into domain-scoped folders with smaller files, while preserving all current behavior after the merged REST-facade and MCP hardening work.

## Why this exists
The branch now has the correct architecture: CLI and MCP share `src/api.ts` as a typed REST facade, while CLI-specific guardrails and MCP-only notification/channel loops stay in their adapters. The next code-quality improvement is navigability and file size reduction. Current hotspots are `src/mcp.ts` (~615 lines), `src/cli.ts` (~487 lines), and `src/api.ts` (~325 lines). The files mix multiple logical concerns, making future edits and reviews harder than necessary.

## Target shape
Keep compatibility entrypoints so existing commands, tests, docs, and installed MCP commands do not break:

```text
src/api.ts -> re-export from src/api/index.ts
src/cli.ts -> run src/cli/index.ts
src/mcp.ts -> re-export/create server from src/mcp/index.ts

src/api/
  index.ts
  types.ts
  peers.ts
  inbox.ts
  events.ts
  groups.ts
  media.ts
  status.ts

src/cli/
  index.ts
  flags.ts
  identity.ts
  warnings.ts
  commands/
    status.ts
    top.ts
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
  render/
    summary.ts
    table.ts

src/mcp/
  index.ts
  server.ts
  state.ts
  notifications.ts
  codex-notifier.ts
  claude-subscription.ts
  lifecycle.ts
  tools/
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
    diagnostics.ts
```

This exact shape can be adjusted during implementation if the code suggests a simpler boundary, but the compatibility entrypoints and behavior preservation are non-negotiable.

## Important constraints
- Do not change public CLI command names, JSON output shapes, MCP tool names, or REST behavior as part of this refactor.
- Do not move CLI guardrails into the shared API layer. CLI identity checks and warnings remain CLI concerns.
- Do not move MCP notification/channel/subscription loops into the shared API layer. MCP runtime behavior remains MCP concern.
- `src/api/*` should remain a typed REST operation facade only.
- Run a verification loop after each child issue, not only at the end.

## Manual verification prerequisite
Before implementation begins, run the current merged branch manually with several agents and confirm existing behavior still works. This is intentionally a separate child issue so the code refactor starts from a trusted baseline.
acceptance_criteria: Manual baseline verification is captured before implementation; API, CLI, and MCP modules are split by domain behind compatibility entrypoints; each stage passes typecheck and focused tests; final branch passes full test suite and MCP e2e.
notes: User highlighted daemon.ts should also be considered. Include daemon modularization as a later phase because src/daemon.ts is currently the largest file (~1077 lines) and mixes route dispatch, validation, persistence helpers, subscription fanout, media-store filesystem behavior, and server startup. Daemon split should happen after API/CLI/MCP adapter splits so behavioral surface is already stable.

title: Document resolved Claude channel delivery bug
description:
## Summary
Claude-to-Claude synchronize DMs were persisted and marked delivered, but the recipient Claude session did not visibly receive a channel auto-prompt.

## Symptoms
- Both peers could register as tool=claude through MCP.
- Sending a DM created an events row and recipient inbox row.
- The daemon callback path marked delivered_at and advanced last_cursor, proving daemon-to-MCP callback delivery.
- Claude UI still did not surface the channel message.

## Root causes found
- synchronize emitted notifications/claude/channel but did not provide Claude channel handling instructions comparable to the working claude-peers MCP reference.
- Claude channel meta payload differed from claude-peers: numeric and nullable fields plus meta.source could plausibly be rejected or ignored when rendered as channel attributes.
- delivered_at only proved local callback success, not Claude UI surfacing.
- In-memory callback subscriptions were lost on daemon restart and were not restored by heartbeat.
- MCP lifecycle cleanup and heartbeat were weaker than claude-peers.
- CLI fallback remained valid but did not explicitly warn agents that CLI peers cannot provide Claude channel real-time prompts.

## Solution implemented
- Added MCP server instructions telling Claude how to react to <channel source="synchronize" ...> and reply with bridge_dm.
- Normalized Claude channel metadata to reference-compatible string-only fields, omitted nulls, removed meta.source, and added from_id/sent_at aliases.
- Added detailed stderr logs in client, daemon, and MCP adapter for daemon discovery, registration, subscriptions, event storage, callback delivery, notification emission, heartbeat, and cleanup.
- Added Claude callback emission failure handling: MCP callback returns 502 if notification emission fails, so daemon does not mark delivered_at as success.
- Added MCP heartbeat and Claude re-subscribe/peer restore logic after daemon restart.
- Added cleanup on stdio close/SIGTERM for both src/mcp.ts and bin/synchronize-mcp.
- Added bridge_whoami diagnostics for notify_mode, subscription state, notifier state, and heartbeat state.
- Added CLI fallback warnings on register/send actions explaining that real-time Claude channel prompts do not work through CLI peers and inbox polling/checking is required.

## Verification
- TypeScript typecheck passed.
- Focused MCP and messaging tests passed.
- Claude MCP was reinstalled to /Users/abhirupdas/.bun/bin/synchronize-mcp with SYNCHRONIZE_MCP_MODE=claude.
- Runtime was reset and claude mcp get synchronize reported Status: Connected.
- User live-tested after the fixes and reported: Works great now.
acceptance_criteria: Resolved and documented. Code contains tests for reference-compatible Claude channel payload, MCP channel instructions, CLI fallback warnings, and MCP delivery paths.
close_reason: Resolved by Claude channel hardening and documentation in code/tests/progress log.

title: Future: evolve 'make doctor' into full-fledged synchronize doctor CLI
description:
## Why this issue exists

`make doctor` is the v0 diagnostic surface — a thin shell-script bundle of useful commands. As the synchronize-debugging skill matures and operators rely on it more heavily, the limitations of a Makefile-based diagnostic become apparent:

- No structured output (JSON / machine-readable) for piping to other tools
- No flag-driven filtering ('only show stale peers', 'only show last hour of events')
- No active probing (can it ping the daemon, can it open the DB, can it resolve a host_session_id back to a tmux session)
- No remediation suggestions ('peer X has been soft-deleted for 4h; consider `bd run sync-…`' or 'daemon pid $X is running from worktree Y, not your current worktree')
- No test-mode (run against dev-runtime via flag instead of env-var dance)

The operator already articulated this future state: 'I would want to flesh out the doctor into a full-fledged diagnostic tool to help with debugging, not just a Make command.'

## What needs to be done (when scheduled)

Design and implement `synchronize doctor` as a proper CLI subcommand under `src/cli/commands/`. Likely shape:

- `synchronize doctor` — default human-readable snapshot (superset of `make doctor`)
- `synchronize doctor --json` — machine-readable
- `synchronize doctor peers [--stale] [--soft-deleted]` — peer-focused
- `synchronize doctor events [--since=…] [--group=…]` — event log
- `synchronize doctor probe` — active health probing (ping daemon, open DB, resolve agent_sessions to tmux)
- `synchronize doctor explain <peer-id>` — narrative explanation of a peer's lifecycle from creation to current state
- Cross-references the synchronize-debugging skill output structure so skill stays the canonical reference

## Acceptance criteria

- (Deferred — will be scoped properly when work begins)
- Subcommand surfaces are decided in design phase, not now
- Existing `make doctor` continues to work (likely delegates to the new CLI)

## Dependencies

Depends on sync-11l (basic `make doctor` lands first to validate the diagnostic shape before promoting to a CLI subcommand).

## Notes

Deliberately P3 / deferred. Operator confirmed v0 `make doctor` is sufficient for now; this issue exists so the longer-term vision is not lost.

title: Web UI chat bubbles should size to ~4:1 width:height ratio, not full-row
description:
## Why this issue exists

Currently every chat bubble in the web UI renders as a full-row, flat container regardless of message length. Short messages get the same wide bubble as long messages, which wastes horizontal space and makes the chat feel like a series of long banners rather than a conversation.

## What we want

Make the bubble container adaptively sized so its width-to-height ratio targets roughly 4:1.

- Short messages → narrow, short bubble.
- Long pasted text → wider until it hits the row max-width, then wraps and grows in height — but the wrap point should be chosen so the resulting bubble is close to 4:1.
- Code blocks / preformatted content can override (they need full width to preserve formatting).

## Acceptance criteria

- Single short message (~1 line) renders as a compact bubble, not full-row.
- Medium message (a paragraph) renders at a width/height ratio close to 4:1.
- Very long messages (multi-paragraph) wrap to roughly 4:1 — width grows up to the row max, then height grows.
- Code blocks (```) keep their existing wide formatting.
- Behavior is the same in both ChatView (main channel) and ThreadPane.

## Implementation notes

- Likely lives in web/src/components/MessageRow.tsx and the bubble styles in web/src/components/extra.css.
- Probably a CSS approach: max-width based on content length, or measure-and-set via ResizeObserver. Either works; CSS is simpler if we can get there.
- Mobile/narrow viewports may need a different aspect target — start with desktop and revisit.

title: PATCH /peers/:id endpoint for lease and metadata updates
description:
## Why this issue exists

The only way to update a peer's lease_expires_at is via the heartbeat endpoint (PATCH /peers/:id/heartbeat), which hard-codes DEFAULT_LEASE_MS. There is no REST API to set a custom or infinite lease, nor to patch other peer metadata (purpose, etc.).

Currently the only workaround is a direct SQLite UPDATE, which is not suitable for normal operation or tooling.

## What needs to be done

Add PATCH /peers/:id accepting a JSON body with optional fields:
- lease_expires_at (ISO string or 'infinite' sentinel)
- purpose (string)

Return the updated peer object.

## Acceptance criteria

- PATCH /peers/:id with {"lease_expires_at": "9999-12-31T23:59:59.000Z"} updates the lease and returns the peer
- PATCH /peers/:id with {"lease_expires_at": "infinite"} sets a far-future date (e.g. year 9999)
- Only the requesting caller's peer or operator-level access can update (non-localhost token required for remote callers)
- Existing heartbeat endpoint is unchanged

title: bridge_list_peers should support include_inactive=true
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: group-scoped bridge_list_peers currently returns only active members. That's the right default, but auditing (reclaim trail, post-rejoin history boundaries) needs left/rejoined members too. Add include_inactive: bool optional param; document active=false rows in response shape.

title: bridge_send_group description should note send-time delivery resolution
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up race-window observation: between her bridge_list_peers and bridge_send_group, karel joined the group. Send-time delivery routing correctly included karel even though pre-send roster didn't. Document explicitly in bridge_send_group description: 'delivery routing is computed at send time, not at roster fetch time' so consumers don't cache rosters incorrectly.

title: Drop recipient_peer_id from group_message event shape
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: recipient_peer_id is always null on group_message events — dead weight on that discriminator. Either omit from group_message responses, or document why it stays (matrix consistency for clients that bind one renderer to all event types). Minor — defer until we revisit the wire shape.

title: Alias-to-peer-id resolution receipt on bridge_send_group
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: mentions: ["612c79ac-..."] tells the sender who got mentioned by peer_id but not which @alias token resolved to which peer. Cheap addition: include resolved: {"@bob": "612c79ac-...", "@karel": "3fe46bbf-..."} on send response. Matters for audit and for cases where multiple members share a session_name across rejoins.

title: Accelerate test suite — currently ~30-47s for ~43 tests
description:
The full `bun test` run takes ~30-47s for ~43 tests in 7 files. Each test that spawns a daemon pays for the full daemon startup cycle (spawn bun, parse src/daemon.ts, open SQLite WAL, write daemon.json, fetch /health). With many such tests, the suite cost is dominated by daemon spinup.

Avenues to research when picking this up:

1. **Daemon reuse across tests in the same file.** Bun's beforeAll/afterAll can spin one daemon per file and have tests use isolated SYNCHRONIZE_HOME per test (by writing to different dirs within the daemon's purview). Catch: most tests assume a clean per-test daemon for fresh state. Would need a per-test reset endpoint or per-test DB.

2. **In-process daemon for tests.** Refactor the daemon entry to export a startDaemon(config) function that runs in the test process (Bun.serve in-memory) rather than spawning a subprocess. Eliminates the bun-startup cost entirely. Big refactor but probably the biggest win.

3. **Parallel safety.** The 2026-05-23 port-pin (DEFAULT_PORT=58405) broke parallel tests because spawned daemons collide. We mitigated with explicit SYNCHRONIZE_PORT=0 in tests but some tests still serialize when they shouldn't.

4. **Profile to find the actual bottleneck.** bun test --timeout / --slow flags may show which tests dominate. Worth measuring before optimizing.

## Acceptance

Target: full suite under 10s on a modern laptop. Should not require sacrificing daemon isolation per test (each test deserves a fresh DB unless the test name says otherwise).

## Background

Observed during the 2026-05-23 v0 manual-test campaign — when iterating on customer-feedback fixes, the 30s suite turnaround broke flow. Filing for later.

title: MCP tool descriptions need explicit Returns: block + per-tool idempotency contract
description: Alice asked for "Returns:" blocks listing response shape (currently must call tool to discover it). Bob asked for explicit per-tool idempotency contract (is bridge_register idempotent? bridge_create_group on existing? bridge_rename_in_group to current alias?). Touches every src/mcp/tools/*.ts description. Bob's idempotency #7 finding. Requires MCP adapter reinstall.

title: bridge_group_history default view gives no signal that thread replies exist
description:
Default `bridge_group_history` returns only main-channel events (`parent_event_id IS NULL`). Per spec, that is correct — Slack-style thread replies should not clutter the main view. But the returned event objects carry no signal that a thread exists for any given main-channel event: no `reply_count`, no `has_replies`, no `last_reply_event_id`. An agent inspecting the response has no cheap way to know threads exist.

## Evidence — live agent confidently misread state

In group `Manual-Test-V0` (13 events, 4 active threads rooted at events 4/6/8/12), the user asked the live Claude agent bob: "How many threads are active?" bob replied: "Zero. Every message so far was sent to the main channel with parent_event_id: null." SQLite cross-check contradicts:

```
sqlite> SELECT event_id, parent_event_id FROM events WHERE parent_event_id IS NOT NULL;
5|4
7|6
9|8
11|8
13|12
```

bob made no factual error in its own reasoning — it just inspected the default history view and saw all-null parent_event_ids. The view itself misled it.

## Why this matters

Agents are the primary consumers of `bridge_group_history`. A response shape that requires per-event probing (`bridge_group_history(thread_of=<id>)` for each main-channel event) to discover threads is asymmetric: cheap for the daemon to provide, expensive for an agent to derive.

## Fix proposals (pick one)

1. **Augment default history rows.** Add `reply_count: integer` and `last_reply_event_id: integer | null` per main-channel event. Single subquery in the existing history handler (`src/daemon.ts` ~line 803).

2. **New `bridge_group_threads({ name })` tool.** Returns `[{ root_event_id, reply_count, last_reply_at }]` — a thread summary endpoint. Keeps history clean; adds an explicit discovery affordance.

Proposal 1 is preferred — single round trip, no new tool surface, agents can discover threads with the same call they already make. Cost: one COUNT/MAX subquery per main-channel row (cheap with the existing thread index).

## Where to consider this

`src/daemon.ts` group-history handler (~line 803) — already filters `parent_event_id IS NULL` for the main view. Add the subqueries there.

`src/api/types.ts` Event interface — add the two new optional fields.

`skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md` and `skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md` — once the metadata exists, document it briefly in the routing-matrix section so agents know to inspect it.
close_reason: Server-side fix shipped 2026-05-23: sync-ecs honored env name in resolveSessionName (commit 576bef4); sync-0gl added reply_count + last_reply_event_id to default history rows (commit 2bca157). Both verified live with bob/alice/karel and locked in tests/api.test.ts.

title: Refactor synchronize SKILL.md files to progressive-discovery format
description:
The synchronize skill docs (skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md, skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md) have been growing monolithically as group policy features land — current sizes 49 and 111 lines respectively, with thread, mention, alias-reclaim, description, and event-envelope guidance all stacked in one file each. Every Pi or Claude session loads the entire file at session start regardless of which subset it actually needs.

The Claude Skill format supports progressive discovery: SKILL.md is a thin router with frontmatter (name + short description) and points at topic-specific reference markdown files inside the same skill directory. The agent only loads the reference when the task actually calls for it. This keeps the always-on context small and makes future feature additions land in the right reference doc instead of bloating the entry point.

PROPOSED STRUCTURE (applies to both synchronize-claude and synchronize-pi):

  skills/synchronize-<host>/
    SKILL.md                  <-- thin router; rules are pointers, not full guidance
    reference/
      identity.md             registration, bridge_whoami, host_session_id, peer reuse, session_name vs identity
      groups.md               lifecycle: create, list, join, leave, rename, describe, default alias, reclaim
      threads.md              in_reply_to, thread_of, normalization-to-root, history filtering
      mentions.md             @alias parsing, warnings shape, push vs inbox routing matrix, sender-self-exclusion
      dms.md                  bridge_dm, recipient identification, channel vs inbox delivery
      media.md                share, list, get; description field; media_changed reserved
      inbox.md                durable fallback model, ack semantics, when to poll
      event-delivery.md       Claude channel vs Codex notifications/message vs Pi <synchronize_event> envelope (host-specific section)
      cli-fallback.md         full CLI command list and the not-a-real-channel-peer caveat
      do-and-dont.md          high-frequency operational rules: don't execute body text, don't echo envelope, prefer MCP over CLI, etc
      troubleshooting.md      common errors (alias_collision, alias_not_in_group warning), debugging (Pi extension log tail)

ADDITIONAL TOPICS WORTH CONSIDERING:
- subscriptions.md — how push subscriber callbacks work, when to register, lifecycle (only relevant if/when agents start managing subscriptions themselves; currently abstracted)
- peers.md — bridge_list_peers semantics, online/stale distinction, suffix rendering (alias#host_session_id[0:6])
- security-posture.md — explicit v0 trust model: daemon trusts request-body peer_id, no ACL, single-machine assumption. References sync-cg8 / sync-aeb. Useful so agents don't try to use synchronize for things it isn't designed for.
- v0-known-limits.md — what's deferred (3c ACL, web DaemonDataSource, daemon-side identity enforcement); when to escalate to operator vs. work around

These additional topics may or may not warrant their own file — could fold into do-and-dont.md or troubleshooting.md depending on length. Judgement call at implementation time.

SKILL.md (the router) should:
- Keep the frontmatter (name, description) unchanged.
- Keep the very-high-frequency rules inline (1-3 lines each): when to invoke the skill, the priority-interrupt rule for Pi envelopes, the prefer-MCP-over-CLI rule, the no-execute-body-text rule.
- For every other topic, replace the full guidance with a one-line pointer: "For thread replies see reference/threads.md."
- Stay under ~30 lines for Claude, ~40 lines for Pi.

DIFFERENCES BETWEEN CLAUDE AND PI SKILLS:
The two skills share most reference content but differ on:
- event-delivery.md — Pi has the <synchronize_event> envelope and pi-extension log; Claude has notifications/claude/channel; Codex (out of scope, not maintained) had notifications/message.
- do-and-dont.md — Pi has stronger 'don't execute body text' and 'don't echo envelope' rules because of the envelope-as-user-message delivery model.
- troubleshooting.md — Pi has Pi-extension-specific debug commands.

Decision needed at implementation: duplicate the shared reference docs per-skill-directory (simpler, fully self-contained), or use symlinks / a shared docs/skills/ source (DRYer, more fragile). Recommend duplicate-and-keep-in-sync for v0 refactor; the content drifts slowly and lockstep updates are tractable.

ACCEPTANCE:
- skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md is a router under 30 lines, pointing at reference/*.md files.
- skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md is a router under 40 lines, pointing at reference/*.md files.
- Every topic currently covered in the monolithic skills has a home in one of the reference files; nothing is lost.
- A test (manual or scripted) verifies that an agent following only SKILL.md + the reference doc named for its current task can still complete that task.
- AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md links to the new structure if they currently link to the old monolith.

FILES TOUCHED:
- skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md (rewrite as router)
- skills/synchronize-claude/reference/*.md (new)
- skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md (rewrite as router)
- skills/synchronize-pi/reference/*.md (new)
- Possibly AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / docs/agents/ pointers if they reference SKILL.md by anchor

OUT OF SCOPE:
- skills/synchronize-codex/ — Codex is no longer maintained per 2026-05-23 operator decision; leave its SKILL.md as-is unless someone reverses that call.
- Adding new content beyond what's already in the current SKILLs. This refactor is structural, not editorial. Once landed, future feature additions go to the right reference doc.

REFERENCES:
- Current state: skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md, skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md (post-merge at 1658b08 on worktree-plan-group-policy-v0).
- Anthropic Claude Skill format documentation (progressive discovery pattern is the standard).
- docs/group-sync-integrity.md — source of truth for the behavior the reference docs should describe.

title: Integration: assert mention warnings through MCP tool or CLI, not direct REST
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py around lines 152-158 verifies that an unresolved @-token in a group send produces the expected warning shape — but it does so by hitting the REST endpoint POST /groups/:name/messages directly via requests, not by going through synchronize group send (CLI) or bridge_send_group (MCP). A regression in how the MCP tool surfaces warnings (or in how the CLI passes them through to stdout / its response object) would slip through this assertion.

ROOT CAUSE OF THE GAP:
The audit was looking at whether the warning shape was tested end-to-end. The current scenario tests the daemon's response shape, but the daemon side is already covered by tests/api.test.ts. The integration value is specifically the surface-level passthrough: daemon → REST → MCP/CLI → caller. That last hop is the only thing this scenario adds, and it is bypassed.

SUGGESTED FIX:
Keep the direct-REST check (defense in depth is fine, and it documents the wire format), but ADD one assertion that captures the warning via the agent-visible surface:
- For the CLI variant: invoke synchronize group send NAME 'hi @ghost' --as alice. The CLI prints response.event and warnings (or should). Parse stdout JSON and assert warnings[0] == {token: '@ghost', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}.
- For the MCP variant (optional, in pi_mcp_group_policy.py): inspect the Pi transcript for the bridge_send_group tool_result content and assert it contains the warning object.

If the CLI does not currently print the warnings array (it prints only response.event per the existing src/cli/commands/group.ts handler), THIS ISSUE ALSO FILES A BUG: the CLI should print warnings somewhere visible. Either:
- Print warnings to stderr as a one-line summary ('warning: @ghost is not a member of group').
- Include warnings in the JSON output by default.
- Add --json-warnings flag.
Choose whichever fits the existing CLI ergonomics.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary.
- src/cli/commands/group.ts — if a CLI fix is needed to make warnings visible.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon strips warnings.
- Scenario fails if REST drops the warnings field.
- Scenario fails if the MCP tool's text() wrapper eats the warnings.
- Scenario fails if the CLI hides warnings from its output.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: make loop guard in pi_mcp_thread_baton robust to prompt-template drift
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py around lines 232-246 implements its anti-cyclic-reply guard by checking whether the harness's run_id substring shows up in recent group event bodies. The intent is: if agents keep re-treating each other's test messages as new instructions, the run_id stays in the body and we can spot the loop.

PROBLEM:
If the prompt template ever stops echoing the run_id (refactor of the warmup prompt, model that does not preserve the sentinel, prompt-truncation by a model that hits context limits), the guard fails silent — it stops detecting loops rather than failing loudly. Worst-case: a cyclic-reply storm runs to harness timeout, costing test minutes and noise, and the failure is mysterious because the loop guard didn't catch what it was designed to catch.

SUGGESTED FIX (two layers; recommend both):
- Layer A (contract): make the run_id echo an explicit prompt-template requirement. If the agent's first send into the group does not contain a sentinel of the form <run_id=...>, the harness fails fast with a clear error 'agent did not echo run_id sentinel; loop guard would be silent — aborting'. This makes the contract impossible to silently break.
- Layer B (backstop): add a body-repetition counter independent of run_id format. Maintain a sliding window of the last N event bodies in the group. If the same body (or alternating bodies) repeats more than M times within a small time window, raise a loop alert and capture the offending event IDs. This catches loops even if Layer A's contract is violated.

POSSIBLE EXTRACTION:
Layer B is generic — extract to scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py as detect_reply_loop(client, group_id, window=10, threshold=4, period_seconds=30). Other group-flavored scenarios can opt in.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — primary.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py — for the shared helper.

ACCEPTANCE:
- If an agent enters a reply loop, the harness fails loudly within a bounded number of iterations, regardless of run_id format.
- If the run_id sentinel is missing from the first send, Layer A fails the scenario immediately.
- Both layers can be exercised by a targeted unit-test-style probe (synthetic group events written via REST).

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: tighten forbidden-tool detection in pi_mcp_thread_baton
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py around lines 248-265 detects 'agent used a forbidden tool' by substring-matching the tool name against the serialized transcript JSON. False-positive risk: any time a forbidden tool name appears anywhere in the transcript text (skill documentation injected via system prompt, model 'thinking' content, error message strings, user prompt echoes, even other tool-result content quoting the forbidden tool's name) the scenario fails spuriously and the harness wastes time chasing a phantom.

Real-world example of how this can bite: if the scenario adds a prompt that says 'Do not use synchronize CLI', the substring 'synchronize CLI' appears in the assistant's response acknowledging the constraint, and the detector trips.

SUGGESTED FIX:
- Parse the Pi JSONL transcript line by line.
- For each row, decode the JSON and look only at role=assistant entries.
- Within each assistant entry, iterate over content blocks.
- Match only block.type == 'tool_use' AND block.name == forbidden_tool_name.

This makes the detector structural rather than textual.

POSSIBLE EXTRACTION:
If multiple scenarios end up needing structured transcript inspection (very likely as the harness grows), extract a helper into scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py — something like extract_tool_uses(transcript_path) -> list[(timestamp, tool_name, args)]. Then scenarios can filter that list cleanly.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — primary.
- Possibly scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py — if a shared helper is extracted.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Detector stops triggering on incidental string matches inside transcripts.
- Detector still catches genuine forbidden tool_use invocations.
- A targeted test: add a prompt mentioning the forbidden tool's name without invoking it; scenario should NOT flag a forbidden-tool violation.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: stale @-mention of a since-left member does not push (D7)
description:
The daemon's mention resolver only matches active members (resolveMentions in src/daemon.ts uses WHERE active=1). If alice leaves and someone later types '@alice', the daemon should:
- Resolve no peer for the token (alice is inactive)
- Return warnings: [{token: '@alice', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}]
- Not push to alice
- Not include alice's peer_id in mentions_json
- Still deliver the message to remaining active members via the normal inbox path

A regression where the resolver matches inactive rows would push to a left peer (or worse, write alice's peer_id into mentions_json on a message that was sent after she left). Pure unit tests cover the resolver behavior but not the surface-level absence-of-push proof.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice and Bob join group; both register with the daemon.
2. (Optional) Register a localhost HTTP callback for alice and bob via POST /subscriptions to capture per-peer push counts.
3. Alice leaves the group via synchronize group leave --as alice.
4. Bob sends 'checking on @alice' via synchronize group send (or bridge_send_group from Pi).
5. Assert the send response includes warnings: [{token: '@alice', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}].
6. Assert event.mentions_json is null or [] (no peer_id resolved).
7. Assert alice's push-sink hit count for the new event_id is 0.
8. Assert alice's inbox does NOT contain the new event (she is not an active member).

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — cleanest path, deterministic CLI flow.
- If a push-sink helper is added for D4, reuse it here.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever pushes to inactive members for any reason.
- Scenario fails if mentions_json starts including inactive peer_ids.
- Warning shape and absence-of-push are both asserted.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts covers the warning-shape assertion for unresolved tokens. This integration scenario adds: (a) the surface-level proof via real CLI/MCP, and (b) the explicit 'no push to left peer' claim that is currently only implicit in the active-members filter.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: directly assert thread-reply push fanout reaches root_author + thread_posters + new mentions (D4)
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py IMPLICITLY exercises the thread-reply push fanout rule — beta and gamma react to a no-mention validation event in the thread, which means push reached them (since they were not mentioned, only inbox+push from thread participation could have woken them). But the harness does not directly assert WHICH peers received the event. A regression that breaks computeThreadParticipants (the daemon helper at src/daemon.ts that computes root_author + prior thread posters) could pass the scenario's behavior check if beta/gamma happen to retry via inbox polling, masking the push regression.

ROUTING RULE BEING TESTED (from docs/group-sync-integrity.md):
thread reply push fanout = (root_author ∪ thread_posters ∪ this-message-mentions) ∩ active_members − {sender}

REPRO SKETCH:
After the alpha-beta-gamma baton sequence, before prompting beta/gamma to acknowledge the validation event, the harness should:

1. Fetch GET /peers/<alpha.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (alpha = root author).
2. Fetch GET /peers/<beta.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (beta = thread poster).
3. Fetch GET /peers/<gamma.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (gamma = thread poster).
4. Negative check: confirm a non-thread-participant (if any exist in the scenario) does NOT have the validation event.

Optional stronger check: register a localhost HTTP callback per peer before the validation send and assert per-peer push hit counts. Inbox-presence is the cheap sufficient signal; subscriber-hit-counts is the proof that PUSH (not just inbox fanout) reached them. Choose based on harness budget.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — between the validation send and the acknowledgment prompts.
- May want a small helper in scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py for 'wait for event_id in peer inbox'.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if computeThreadParticipants regresses to mentions-only, root-only, or otherwise drops a participant.
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever stops fanning out to thread posters.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts has push-sink-based assertions of the same rule at the daemon layer ('thread reply push reaches root author and prior thread posters along with new mentions'). The integration version adds the MCP-driven-from-real-Pi proof — useful because real Pi exercises the subscriber path differently from a synthetic Bun.serve sink.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: assert fresh=true history boundary for late joiners (B11)
description:
history_from_event_id is set at join time per member (UPSERT in src/daemon.ts join handler). A fresh=true joiner is supposed to see only post-join events; a default joiner sees the full group history. This is agent-visible behavior — a Pi agent joining fresh=true mid-conversation should not surface old messages.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice creates group, joins as 'alice'.
2. Alice sends 'pre-bob' message.
3. Bob joins with --fresh on the CLI (fresh: true MCP arg).
4. Alice sends 'post-bob' message.
5. GET /groups/:name/history?peer_id=<bob>. Assert 'pre-bob' is ABSENT, 'post-bob' is PRESENT in the events array.
6. Carol joins WITHOUT --fresh.
7. GET /groups/:name/history?peer_id=<carol>. Assert BOTH 'pre-bob' and 'post-bob' are present.

This proves three things at once: (a) fresh joiner is cut at the join boundary, (b) default joiner gets full history, (c) the per-member history_from_event_id read-time filter actually runs.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — natural home. Existing scenario already exercises joins + sends; just thread in the --fresh case.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever stops honoring fresh=true at the read path.
- Scenario fails if the CLI or MCP forwarder drops the fresh flag.
- Scenario passes against current daemon (integration tip b7f86cf).

RELATED UNIT TEST:
None directly. tests/messaging.test.ts has a 'before joins' assertion that touches related behavior but only loosely — it doesn't isolate fresh=true vs default joiner. This integration scenario plus a future api.test.ts unit pair would close the loop.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: alias-collision 409 surfaces as actionable error to agent (B7)
description:
When two active members try to claim the same alias, the daemon returns 409 alias_collision with a hint instructing the caller to pick a unique alias (string 'Provide a unique alias to join this group.' in src/daemon.ts). The error must traverse the chain:

  daemon → REST 409 → MCP tool / CLI → agent-readable surface

A regression that downgrades the response to a 500, strips the message body, swallows the rejection, or maps it to a misleading error code would not be caught by tests/api.test.ts (which asserts the daemon layer only).

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice joins durable group with --alias lead.
2. Bob attempts to join the same group with --alias lead.
3. Assert the request fails.
4. Assert HTTP status is 409.
5. Assert error code is alias_collision.
6. Assert body contains the substring 'unique alias' (the agent-visible hint).

Suggested second variant: do the same flow via the Pi MCP path (bridge_join_group) in pi_mcp_group_policy.py to prove the MCP tool transport preserves the error shape.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary, deterministic.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_group_policy.py — optional second variant via Pi MCP.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario verifies the full error chain end-to-end, not just the daemon-side rejection.
- Scenario fails if the surfaces ever swallow, downgrade, or rephrase the rejection.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts asserts daemon-level rejection via direct REST. Integration adds the surface-translation layer (REST → MCP/CLI → agent transcript).

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: assert bridge_join_group default-alias path (B6)
description:
Every join in scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py currently passes an explicit --alias flag. The MCP tool surface and CLI both promise that omitting alias defaults to the registered session_name (handled in daemon's POST /groups/:name/join: 'const alias = optionalString(body, \"alias\") ?? peer.session_name'). Only an end-to-end scenario can prove the default propagates through bridge_join_group / synchronize group join.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Register a peer with session_name = \"alice\" (already happens during harness setup).
2. Call bridge_join_group (Pi) or synchronize group join without an alias.
3. Read back via GET /groups/:name/members.
4. Assert alias == \"alice\".

A second variant should pass alias explicitly (already in the existing scenario), so a comparative assertion is straightforward.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary home, deterministic and fast.
- The existing assert_active_alias helper (group_policy_cli.py) can be reused.
- Optional: add to scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_group_policy.py too if you want surface coverage via real Pi MCP.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if a future regression silently writes alias = NULL, an empty string, or a different default.
- Scenario passes against the daemon at integration tip (b7f86cf or later).

RELATED UNIT TEST:
None. Existing tests/api.test.ts unit tests assert the daemon-side default, but not the CLI/MCP forwarding path. This is the integration value.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: [epic] Integration harness coverage gaps for group policy v0
description:
The integration harness at `scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/` (added on branch `group-policy-v0-additional-testing`, commits 02ee16d + 194373f) gives high-confidence end-to-end coverage of the agent-visible group-policy v0 behaviors enumerated in `docs/group-sync-integrity.md`. An independent audit (2026-05-23) mapped each scenario assertion against the 27-item invariant checklist:

WELL COVERED (14 items, PASS at integration level):
- A1, A2 identity registration + bridge_whoami
- B1, B2 group create/describe with description
- B5 explicit-alias join
- B8 alias freed on leave
- B9 reclaim audit event (with peer_id + previous_peer_id assertions)
- B10 self-scoped rename
- C1, C2, C3 thread send + reply normalization (reply-to-reply collapses to root)
- C5, C6 history filter (default excludes replies; thread_of returns root + replies in order)
- D1, D2 mention resolution + warnings for unresolved tokens
- D5 sender does not get push for own message
- D6 (partial) roster events land in members' inboxes
- E2 host_session_id surfacing on group roster

GAPS WORTH FILLING AT INTEGRATION LEVEL (5 items):
Five v0 behaviors are agent-visible across the CLI/MCP surface in a way only an end-to-end test can prove. Child issues track each:
- B6 alias defaults to session_name when omitted
- B7 alias-collision (409) reaches agent as readable error
- B11 fresh=true history boundary for late joiners
- D4 thread reply push fanout direct assertion (root_author ∪ thread_posters ∪ new mentions)
- D7 stale @-mention of a since-left member must not push

BRITTLE ASSERTIONS TO TIGHTEN (3 items):
Three existing assertions could silently break or false-positive. Child issues track each:
- forbidden-tool detection by JSON string search in pi_mcp_thread_baton.py
- loop guard depends on run_id echoing in message bodies
- warning-shape assertion in group_policy_cli.py bypasses the MCP layer

GAPS DELIBERATELY NOT FILED (covered by tests/api.test.ts):
These are pure daemon-level invariants. Duplicating them in tmux/Pi adds runtime cost without finding new bugs — the unit suite already gates regressions at the storage and route layer.
- A3 CLI peer reuse (existing resolveCliRegisterPeerId + findReusablePeer logic)
- B3 case-insensitive name collision
- B4 ephemeral group + media_dir purge on daemon restart
- C4 in_reply_to targeting an event in a different group
- C7 thread_of pointing at a non-root reply
- C8 thread_of pointing at a non-existent event_id
- E1 events.type CHECK constraint

NOT A V0 BLOCKER:
The harness as it stands is sufficient for the v0 bundle merge to master. This epic gathers improvements; it does not gate shipping. Run only when prioritized.

REFERENCE:
- docs/group-sync-integrity.md — checklist of v0 invariants and the routing matrix
- session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md — design plan
- scripts/README.md — harness usage notes
- .claude/handoffs/2026-05-23-group-policy-v0-phases-1-2-dx2.md — decision log + audit context
- sync-l91 (closed) — the group-policy v0 epic this audits

SUCCESSOR / RELATED:
- sync-aeb (P3, deferred) — ACL/blocks, also not in the harness, but explicitly out of v0 scope.
- sync-jix — DaemonDataSource, gates any web-UI-level integration tests.

title: Add Pi integration harness tool allow/deny policy enforcement
description: Design and implement a policy layer for real Pi integration harness runs that can declare which MCP tools an agent is allowed to use for each prompt/phase and which tools or surfaces should fail the run if used. The immediate motivation is the thread-baton scenario: agents should prove mention and thread push semantics by acting from injected synchronize_event envelopes, not by discovering state through history reads. Today the harness can inspect transcripts after the fact, but it cannot systematically enforce a per-phase tool policy or explain violations cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Harness supports per-agent/per-phase allowed tool declarations for Pi prompts; violations are detected deterministically from Pi transcripts or structured logs; failures include the agent, phase, forbidden tool, and relevant transcript excerpt; policy can distinguish setup tools from workflow tools; docs explain when to use policy enforcement versus prompt wording; at least one real Pi scenario uses the policy to prevent history/discovery reads during event-driven baton replies.

title: Improve Pi tmux capture parsing with verbose tool logs
description:
What/why/how:
Pi tmux pane captures currently hide long MCP tool calls and tool results behind compact UI sections such as the Ctrl+O expand directive. The most important integration-test evidence lives inside MCP JSON calls/results and injected <synchronize_event> envelopes, but the pane capture is hard to parse deterministically in this compact mode. Investigate launching Pi with verbose output for integration tests, then add a parser that extracts structured MCP call/result records and synchronize event sections from each Pi agent log.

Impact area:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py Pi launch command, likely adding --verbose for integration runs.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/tmux.py or a new diagnostics parser module.
- scripts/README.md and docs/integration-tmux.md diagnostics guidance.

Acceptance criteria:
- Integration Pi launch can run in verbose mode so pane captures include full MCP tool calls/results instead of collapsed Ctrl+O sections.
- A deterministic parser extracts MCP call names, JSON arguments, JSON results, and injected synchronize_event envelopes from captured panes or transcripts.
- Failure artifacts include a structured parsed log per Pi agent in addition to raw pane capture.
- Existing real Pi DM and group-policy smoke tests continue to pass.

How to verify:
- Run a real Pi integration smoke and confirm artifacts include full parsed MCP entries for bridge_whoami/list/send/history calls.
- Confirm captures no longer require manual Ctrl+O expansion for the primary evidence.

Notes:
Keep this as diagnostics-only work; it should not change product behavior or the semantic assertions of the integration scenarios.

title: Migrate ContextMenu.tsx to @radix-ui/react-context-menu

Verify Radix portals respect [data-theme=...] cascading — render a context menu under both light and dark themes and confirm tokens still apply. This is the PROOF POINT for the Radix approach; if the visual / theme cascade / Vim-nav-compatibility checks all pass, R2/R3 follow. If any of them fail, this issue documents the blocker and the rest of the epic is reconsidered.
acceptance_criteria: (1) ContextMenu.tsx uses Radix primitives; bespoke focus/outside-click/portal code removed. (2) All 4 consumer surfaces (sidebar, message, roster, timeline — per DESIGN.md) still work. (3) Visual: zero change vs pre-Radix baseline. (4) Theme cascade: open menu under light, swap to dark — menu re-skins. (5) Keyboard: Esc closes + returns focus; arrows navigate items; Enter activates; Tab does not break out. (6) useVimNav still works when the menu is closed. (7) LOC delta noted in bead notes (expect ~-40).
close_reason: Duplicate of sync-ea8 (which already exists with a detailed approach for ContextMenu Radix migration). Decision context recorded in sync-99s notes.

title: Web UI: adopt Radix primitives for accessibility-sensitive widgets
description: Replace bespoke focus / keyboard / portal / ARIA plumbing in ContextMenu, Toast, and the Composer @mention popup with Radix UI primitives. Behavior gets correct-by-default (focus return, roving focus, ARIA-live, Esc/click-outside semantics); visual aesthetic is unchanged (Radix is headless — we keep our existing CSS classes as the skin layer). LOC delta projected by audit: ~150 LOC removed from the three components, +3 small Radix deps. THIS EPIC IS DEFERRED behind sync-75p (tokenization). Rationale: the value of Radix-as-behavior-only is amplified when the styling layer is already cleanly tokenized — re-skinning Radix widgets per future theme is then a pure CSS exercise. Doing Radix first risks tangling visual + behavioral cleanup in the same diffs.
design: Approach: Radix headless primitives, NOT shadcn/ui. shadcn ships with Tailwind + a specific visual aesthetic; we want only the behavior layer. Deps to add: @radix-ui/react-context-menu, @radix-ui/react-toast, @radix-ui/react-popover (and possibly @radix-ui/react-collection for the mention list, or hand-roll a list with arrow-key handling). Each Radix component is rendered with our existing className values intact, so the CSS sweep done in sync-75p continues to drive the look. Each widget gets its own bead so they can land independently; ContextMenu first because it's the smallest and most contained (88 LOC -> ~40 LOC projected). Risk: theme attribute interaction with Radix portals — Radix portals render to document.body, so :root[data-theme=...] must continue to cascade. Confirm during ContextMenu prototype before expanding.
acceptance_criteria: (1) ContextMenu, Toast, and Composer @mention popup all use Radix primitives. (2) Visual diff vs pre-Radix baseline = zero (same skin, same CSS). (3) Keyboard semantics improved: Esc closes + returns focus; Tab/Shift-Tab navigates without breaking out; arrow keys work in menus and mention list; screen reader announces toasts. (4) Vim nav (useVimNav) continues to function — Radix widgets do not capture keys outside their open state. (5) bun test + bun run typecheck green. (6) LOC delta documented; expected ~-150 net.
close_reason: Duplicate Radix execution epic; sync-99s is the canonical epic. All children re-filed or closed-as-dup. See sync-99s decision note for context.
